title: Fractions
objective: Students will explore fractions concepts including Tenths, Fractions on a number line, Splitting shapes into equal parts (age 7+) and more.

steps.content: Today we're going to learn about Tenths. Get ready for an adventure!
Count up and down in tenths; recognise that tenths arise from dividing an object into 10 equal parts and from dividing one-digit numbers or quantities by 10
Let's practice together! Count: one tenth, two tenths, three tenths … up to ten tenths (one whole)
If you splits a chocolate bar with 10 equal pieces, can they tell you each piece is one tenth — and count up from 1/10 to 10/10 in order?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Tenths. Count: one tenth, two tenths, three tenths … up to ten tenths (one whole) Show that dividing a shape into 10 equal parts gives tenths
Today we're going to learn about Fractions on a number line. Get ready for an adventure!
Recognise and use fractions as numbers: place unit fractions and non-unit fractions with small denominators on a number line
Time to try this out: Place 1/2, 1/4, 3/4 on a number line from 0 to 1
If you draw a number line from 0 to 1, can you mark where 1/4, 1/2, and 3/4 should go — without any extra help?
Great job! Today we learned about Fractions on a number line. Place 1/2, 1/4, 3/4 on a number line from 0 to 1 Identify that 1/3 lies between 0 and 1/2 on the number line
Let's explore Splitting shapes into equal parts (age 7+)! Have you ever wondered about this?
Partition circles and rectangles into two, three, or four equal shares; describe shares as halves, thirds, and fourths; recognise that equal shares of identical wholes need not have the same shape
Time to try this out: Partition a circle into 3 equal parts and label each 'a third'
If you cut a rectangle into thirds in two different ways (three strips vs three squares), can you recognise that both give equal thirds — even though the pieces look different?

Great job! Today we learned about Splitting shapes into equal parts (age 7+). Partition a circle into 3 equal parts and label each 'a third' Partition a rectangle into 4 equal shares in more than one way
Let's explore Equivalent fractions! Have you ever wondered about this?
Recognise and show, using diagrams, equivalent fractions with small denominators
Time to try this out: Show that 1/2 = 2/4 using a diagram of equal parts
If you sees a bar divided into 4 equal parts with 2 shaded, can they tell you that's the same as 1/2 — and draw a different bar showing the same amount another way?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Equivalent fractions. Show that 1/2 = 2/4 using a diagram of equal parts Use a fraction wall or bar model to find equivalent fractions
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Simple Fraction Sums!
Add and subtract fractions with the same denominator within one whole (e.g. 5/7 + 1/7 = 6/7)
Let me show you something cool. Calculate 2/5 + 2/5 = 4/5
If a recipe calls for 2/8 of a cup of milk and then another 3/8 of a cup, can you work out the total without a calculator?
Great job! Today we learned about Simple Fraction Sums. Calculate 2/5 + 2/5 = 4/5 Calculate 6/8 − 3/8 = 3/8
Today we're going to learn about Comparing fractions. Get ready for an adventure!
Compare and order unit fractions, and fractions with the same denominator
Let me show you something cool. Order 1/2, 1/3, 1/4, 1/5 from largest to smallest
If you has eaten 3/8 of a cake and a friend has eaten 5/8 of the same cake, can they tell you who has eaten more — and put those fractions in order from smallest to biggest?
Great job! Today we learned about Comparing fractions. Order 1/2, 1/3, 1/4, 1/5 from largest to smallest Explain that a larger denominator means smaller unit fractions
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Unit fractions!
Recognise, find, and write fractions of a discrete set of objects: unit fractions and non-unit fractions with small denominators
Time to try this out: Find 1/4 of 12 objects by dividing into 4 equal groups
If there are 15 books on a shelf, can you work out how many two-thirds of them is — and check by grouping the books into three equal piles?
Great job! Today we learned about Unit fractions. Find 1/4 of 12 objects by dividing into 4 equal groups Find 3/4 of 12 objects
Let's discover the amazing world of Comparing fractions (age 7+)!
Solve problems involving counting in tenths, fractions of quantities, equivalence, fraction addition/subtraction, and fraction comparison
Let me show you something cool. Solve a word problem requiring finding a fraction of a quantity
If a bag of 20 sweets is shared equally among 5 friends, can you work out what fraction each person gets — and how many sweets that is?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Comparing fractions (age 7+): Solve a word problem requiring finding a fraction of a quantity Solve a problem that requires comparing or ordering fractions
